issues.title: Directorate Members in Path 2
issues.author: martinduke
issues.body:
Members of directorates have been vetted by leadership and (if the directorate is managed correctly) have been actively contributing reviews.

We could add "directorate member" to Path 2, maybe if they've been in good standing for 2 years.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body: This seems like a good thing to me.

comments.author: larseggert
comments.body: Do we mean directorates specifically, or also (some of) the other types of groups we have? See https://datatracker.ietf.org/group/

comments.author: martinduke
comments.body: I don't have a strong opinion about other groups, but to me the criteria are that they (1) do work and (2) their membership is vetted in some way (i.e. you can be fired for not doing anything)

comments.author: martinduke
comments.body: As discussed at the interim, I don't think this makes any sense given our full inclusion of remote participants. No one spoke up for this at the meeting so I'm closing it.

comments.author: martinduke
comments.body: If there are any suggestions for what to include, (or better yet actual data to contribute), this is the place.

issues.title: Attendance Criteria is Way Too Lax
issues.author: d3e3e3
issues.body:
While it is true that just picking up your badge counted as "attendance" in the past, in reality the vast majority of "attendees" at IETF meetings attended numerous sessions over several days if not the entire week. Since session attendance was recorded manually on the Blue Sheets, it would have been quite burdensome to actually check the number of sessions someone had attended, or the like.

Now, with hybrid meetings, per session attendance data is all available online. This makes it practical to require larger amount of participation at IETF meetings as part of the criteria for nomcom eligibility. The current criteria in the draft, of checking in to attend one session, is way too lax. A person who habitually attends only one session per IETF meeting or who only attends session of one WG is a very narrow participant in the IETF and should not be eligible for nomcom.

For an example of attendance criteria for a week long meeting that has been used by another standards organization, see Section 7.1 of https://mentor.ieee.org/802.11/dcn/14/11-14-0629-21-0000-802-11-operations-manual.docx which defines attendance as attending for 75% of the session hours during a week. (In 802.11, attendance is used to earn voting rights.) I do not think we need to be that stringent. But I would say that someone who does not attend at least two sessions a day for three days of an IETF meeting week really cannot be reasonably described as having attended that IETF meeting.

comments.author: mstjohns
comments.body:
Yeah - no.   A good portion of the participation that's most useful for a Nomcom participant are the various ad-hoc hallway and bar sessions.   Now I'm not necessarily opposed to including my bar tab and a list of interactees when I volunteer for the Nomcom, but I think that might be difficult to verify and tabulate by a third party.    

WRT to the comparison to an IEEE meeting - I'm not very familiar with the scope of the meeting to which the 75% applies, but from discussions with folk who have attended IEEE as part of their funded lifestyles, that IEEE meeting you're referring to tends to be more closely matched in scope to an IETF working group or small cluster of working groups than to the IETF week-long whole ball of wax.  

I would oppose this change in criteria.  Mike

issues.title: Inferior Working Group Functionaries
issues.author: d3e3e3
issues.body:
Working Group Chairs are substantially involved in IETF processes and are trusted to make consensus judgements. It is reasonable for someone who has served for a reasonable length of time as a Working Group Chair to be eligible to serve on the Nominations Committee, although it appears this will only enable a tiny number of people who would not otherwise qualify.

The same cannot be said of Working Group Secretaries or other Working Group functionaries, such as Technical Advisors or meeting scribes, who can be appointed and removed by the Chair. They should not, merely because they are such WG functionaries, be thereby eligible for Nominations Committee voting membership.

comments.author: martinduke
comments.body: Discussed in London. No one strongly objects to keeping secretaries in.

issues.title: More Security Considerations
issues.author: martinduke
issues.body:
Some things that came up in London:

- a different threat is a single person trying to get on the Nomcom to be disruptive (can they be expelled?)
- Nomcom confirmation
- fee waivers are a lever
-  maybe add an eligibility challenge step?
